title: AI Agent Liability, Insurance, and Attestation Tracker
description: Verified tracker of insurance products, certification schemes, logging and attestation standards, cases, and legal status entries for autonomous AI agent risk: what coverage exists, what evidence insurers and certifiers require, and what the primary sources actually establish.
citation: Vorp Labs, AI Agent Liability, Insurance, and Attestation Tracker (as of 2026-07-21), https://vorplabs.com/ai-agent-liability

records.summary: Agent-specific security, safety, and reliability standard covering data and privacy, security, safety, reliability, accountability, and societal risk. The launch announcement says certification requires 50-plus technical, operational, and legal safeguards plus third-party technical testing.

Certification comprises scoping, evidence collection, technical evaluations, remediation, report sign-off, and certificate issuance. Certificates last 12 months; in-scope agents require quarterly red-teaming to maintain them. AIUC's own pages currently state both four-to-eight and five-to-ten week timelines, an official-source inconsistency this tracker preserves rather than normalizes.

Requires logs of AI processes, actions, and outputs to support incident investigation, audit, and behavior explanation. Expected agent evidence includes provenance metadata, tool-call parameters and results, sub-agent delegations and outcomes, human approval events, and reasoning traces where available, plus retention rules, access controls, PII sanitation, and optional tamper-evidence such as WORM storage and hash chains.
records.sourceTitle: AIUC-1 control E015: log model activity

records.entryName: AIUC-1 C007 and C009 human oversight controls
records.summary: C007 defines high-risk output review workflows with detection thresholds, reviewer assignments, escalation paths, and documented decisions; C009 defines real-time user stop, pause, redirect, feedback, and issue-reporting mechanisms. Both controls are marked optional in the standard.
records.sourceTitle: AIUC-1 control C007: flag high-risk recommendations
records.notes: Optional controls in the standard, not baseline requirements.

records.entryName: AIUC-1 B006 and D003 agent and tool boundaries
records.summary: B006 requires safeguards preventing agents from acting beyond authorized scope, with service and MCP-server allowlists, gateway restrictions, and boundary-violation logs. D003 requires tool authorization and validation with parameter checks, rate limits, transaction caps, circuit breakers, tool-call logs, and optional human approval for sensitive operations.
records.sourceTitle: AIUC-1 control D003: restrict unsafe tool calls

records.summary: Requires independent testing of tool calls at least every three months, with the tested risk taxonomy, methodology, findings, remediation actions, and remediation timelines documented in the report.
records.sourceTitle: AIUC-1 control D004: third-party testing of tool calls

records.summary: Separate failure plans are required for security or privacy breaches, harmful outputs, and hallucinations causing customer financial loss, with owners, notification and remediation paths, preserved logs for legal review, and potential insurer activation. E004 requires accountable approval of material system changes; E008 and E009 cover internal review trails and third-party access monitoring.
records.sourceTitle: AIUC-1 accountability controls: AI failure plans

records.summary: AIUC's homepage describes a quarterly standard update focused on MCP security, third-party risk, and agent identity and permissions. The standard changes quarterly, so control citations in this tracker are re-diffed on that cadence.
records.notes: Event date approximated to the quarter start; AIUC labels it Q2 2026.

records.summary: Markets insurance for AI agents covering AI-specific losses up to $50 million, listing hallucinations, brand risk, data leakage, IP infringement, and tool-call failures such as incorrect refunds or purchase decisions. The launch announcement says cost and availability are tied directly to audit results. Policy forms, exclusions, carrier identity, and territorial details are not publicly disclosed.

ElevenLabs announced it is the first company to go live with an AIUC-1-backed policy covering AI voice agents and their actions, naming incorrect information supplied to a customer as an insurable risk, after 5,835 tests across 14 risk categories. The carrier, policy limit, premium, exclusions, and claims trigger were not disclosed.

records.actor: Armilla with Swiss Re, Greenlight Re, and Chaucer
records.summary: Insurance-backed AI performance warranty based on independent evaluation of performance, fairness, robustness, and security; if a verified algorithm misses warranted KPIs, the buyer can recover the license fee under the warranty terms.

records.actor: Armilla and Lloyd's underwriters including Chaucer
records.summary: Standalone third-party AI liability coverage for legal costs and liabilities arising from underperformance such as hallucinations, inaccuracies, model drift, mechanical failure, and deviations from expected behavior, described at launch as available to US insureds with global territorial limits.

Expanded limits up to $25 million per organization with express coverage descriptions for autonomous-agent failures: incorrect agent decisions, improper tool use, escalation errors, non-breach data leakage, property damage, and regulatory defense. Each policy is described as including independent AI-system certification and risk reporting.
records.notes: The page is dated January 21, 2026 but contains an apparent 2025 dateline typo in its body, preserved here as a source caveat.

records.summary: Coordinates Chaucer cyber and technology E&O with Armilla's standalone AI liability policy, using predefined allocation rules for mixed incidents, with AI liability limits described as $25 million or more per organization. Availability remains subject to underwriting and jurisdiction.

Armilla's broker page says coverage is active in North America, broker-mediated, and typically 12 months, and that quote intake requests prior AI incidents including carrier notification, dates, and remediation steps: prior-incident history is underwriting evidence.

Cyber and technology E&O for AI platforms and products, including AI product liability, generated-media liability, regulatory liability, a discrimination carveback, and deepfake crime. US products are offered through a licensed affiliate and may not be available in every state.
Excess difference-in-conditions wrap for AI-related professional negligence, IP infringement, personal injury, discrimination, privacy, bodily injury, property damage, and regulatory violations.

First-party response cover for organizations using or embedding third-party AI: business interruption, reputational harm, product recall, and response costs. The fetched materials do not disclose agent-specific trace or attestation requirements.

Performance-guarantee insurance for AI providers addressing errors and generative-AI hallucinations, able to support warranties indemnifying customers for financial losses or legal liabilities directly related to AI errors. An AI solution must undergo Munich Re's technical due diligence to obtain coverage; the public summary does not disclose a complete agent-trace data dictionary.
records.notes: Event date approximated to the year Munich Re says the product was pioneered.

records.entryName: HSB AI liability insurance for small businesses
records.summary: Coverage for small and medium businesses for lawsuits involving bodily injury, property damage, and personal or advertising injury arising from AI use. As announced it was pending regulatory approval, to be added to partner carriers' business policies rather than sold directly by HSB.
records.sourceTitle: HSB AI liability insurance press release

records.entryName: Lloyd's recognition of Armilla as an AI-liability MGA
records.summary: Lloyd's describes Armilla as an operating MGA and Lloyd's coverholder dedicated to AI liability, with products addressing algorithmic errors, model drift, and hallucinations: third-party corroboration of the market's existence from the marketplace itself.

Caliber Labs, PBC does business as the Artificial Intelligence Underwriting Company. Its launch announcement identified Rune Kvist, Brandon Wang, and Rajiv Dattani as founders with a $15 million seed led by Nat Friedman at NFDG. The current team page lists Kvist and Dattani; the omission of Wang is preserved as a source-history note.
Cross-sector generative-AI profile of the AI Risk Management Framework, published July 26, 2024. NIST does not characterize it as an agent-specific certification or logging specification.

An initiative for industry-led technical standards, voluntary guidance, protocol interoperability, security, and standards-gap analysis for AI agents. The fetched page describes an initiative, not a completed certifiable agent standard.
records.notes: Event date approximated to the year; NIST's page does not carry a precise date.

records.summary: Work-in-progress specification for instrumentable, traceable, inspectable agents with audit trails, a dynamic bill of materials, and inline controls, building on OpenTelemetry, OCSF, MCP, A2A, CycloneDX, SPDX, and SWID. Its OCSF mapping extends the API Activity class for agent-specific security events.

Armilla announced a live AI litigation database integrated with model evaluation and underwriting. The database endpoint, row schema, update cadence, and public-download terms were not established from the announcement; it is tracked here as the closest adjacent maintained resource.

The tribunal found Air Canada liable in negligent misrepresentation for inaccurate bereavement-fare information given by its website chatbot, holding that the airline is responsible for all the information on its website whether it comes from a static page or a chatbot, and that it did not take reasonable care to ensure its chatbot was accurate. Air Canada was ordered to pay $812.02 CAD in damages, interest, and fees. Neutral citation 2024 BCCRT 149; Tribunal Member Christopher C. Rivers.
records.sourceTitle: Moffatt v. Air Canada, 2024 BCCRT 149 (Civil Resolution Tribunal decisions database)
records.notes: A small-claims tribunal decision from British Columbia; persuasive but not binding precedent elsewhere.

records.entryName: EU AI Liability Directive proposal withdrawn
records.summary: The European Parliament's legislative observatory records procedure 2022/0303(COD), the proposed AI Liability Directive, with status 'Procedure lapsed or withdrawn' and the event 'Proposal withdrawn by Commission' dated October 6, 2025. No successor instrument appears in the procedure file as of the tracker date.

evidenceRequirements.name: End-to-end execution trace
evidenceRequirements.requirement: AIUC-1 E015 calls for process, action, output, and metadata logs; agent evidence includes provenance, tool calls, delegations, approvals, and reasoning traces where available.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Session or run ID; agent ID and version; deployment origin; initiating principal; timestamps; input and output references; processing steps; parent-child spans; termination state.

evidenceRequirements.name: Tool-call audit trail
evidenceRequirements.requirement: AIUC-1 D003 expects logs containing the originating MCP server, tool name and version, parameters, timestamps, unauthorized-access alerts, and scope violations.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Tool and server identity; tool schema or version; sanitized parameters; result or result hash; authorization decision; policy evaluated; retry count; side effects; latency; error; transaction value.

evidenceRequirements.name: Delegation and multi-agent chain
evidenceRequirements.requirement: E015 includes sub-agent handoffs with identity, task context, and outcome at each step.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Parent agent; delegated agent; task; context boundary; delegated permissions; accepted or rejected state; output; return-to-parent event.

evidenceRequirements.name: Human approval record
evidenceRequirements.requirement: E015 and D003 include approval events and optional human approval for sensitive, high-risk, or multi-step operations.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Approver identity and role; timestamp; action preview; amount or risk tier; approve, reject, modify, or timeout result; comments; linked policy; resulting execution ID.

evidenceRequirements.name: High-risk output review
evidenceRequirements.requirement: C007 expects documented risk criteria, detection thresholds, reviewer assignments, escalation paths, queue tracking, SLAs, and decision records. The control is optional in the standard.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Risk category; classifier or rule version; score and threshold; reviewer; queue timestamps; decision; escalation; correction; customer notification.

evidenceRequirements.name: User intervention records
evidenceRequirements.requirement: C009 describes stop, pause, redirect, feedback, and issue-reporting mechanisms. The control is optional in the standard.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Intervention control shown; user action; timestamp; state before and after; whether a pending side effect was cancelled; feedback category.

evidenceRequirements.name: Permission and boundary evidence
evidenceRequirements.requirement: B006 expects API gateway rules, network policies, MCP-server allowlists, service authorization, isolation boundaries, and alerts for boundary violations.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Agent-to-service allowlist; denied endpoints; credential scope; sandbox policy; network policy; MCP registration record; policy changes; violations.

evidenceRequirements.name: Rate and transaction controls
evidenceRequirements.requirement: D003 expects tool quotas, time-window limits, transaction caps, and circuit breakers for autonomous use.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Per-tool limits; per-agent limits; monetary caps; reset windows; circuit-breaker activations; manual overrides; override approver.

evidenceRequirements.name: Independent evaluations
evidenceRequirements.requirement: D004 requires quarterly third-party tool testing with methodology, findings, and tracked remediation; AIUC-1 separately requires quarterly red-teaming to keep certification valid.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Signed report; test environment; agent and model version; scenarios or secured references; severity; reproducibility; owner; due date; retest result.

evidenceRequirements.name: Incident and evidence-preservation plans
evidenceRequirements.requirement: AIUC-1 requires separate plans for breaches, harmful outputs, and hallucinations, with preserved system logs for legal review, customer communication, corrective action, and potential insurer activation.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Incident ID; preserved log snapshot; legal hold; affected runs and users; containment; notification deadlines; insurer notice; counsel and forensic contacts.

evidenceRequirements.name: Change approval trail
evidenceRequirements.requirement: E004 identifies model, meta-prompt, guardrail, workflow, and materially performance-affecting changes as requiring accountable approval and evidence.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Change request; before and after configuration; evaluation delta; approver; sign-off; deployment ID; rollback plan; artifact signature.

evidenceRequirements.name: Third-party access logs
evidenceRequirements.requirement: E009 expects API, session, and data-access logs with identities, authentication times, resource access, session duration, IP, usage patterns, and anomaly alerts.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Vendor or service identity; credentials used; API and endpoint; data class; timestamp; IP; duration; volume; anomaly; responsible reviewer.

evidenceRequirements.name: Retention, access, and integrity controls
evidenceRequirements.requirement: E015 describes retention rules, access controls, PII sanitation, and optional tamper-evidence such as WORM storage, cryptographic hashes, and append-only stores.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Retention schedule by data class; deletion job; authorized viewers; PII masking; encryption; hash chain or immutable store; clock synchronization.

evidenceRequirements.name: Prior-incident underwriting history
evidenceRequirements.requirement: Armilla's broker intake requests previous AI incidents, whether a carrier was notified, dates, and remediation steps.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Incident chronology; root cause; affected model or agent; loss amount; carrier notice; remediation; recurrence testing.

evidenceRequirements.name: Certification and risk report
evidenceRequirements.requirement: Armilla says each expanded policy includes independent AI-system certification and risk reporting; AIUC says insurance terms depend on audit results.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Certificate scope; tested agent and configuration; report date; exceptions; evaluation results; unresolved findings; expiration; recertification date.

evidenceRequirements.name: Munich Re technical due diligence
evidenceRequirements.requirement: Munich Re requires technical due diligence for aiSure coverage, but its public summary does not expose a complete agent-specific evidence schema.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Publicly undisclosed; the tracker records the requirement's existence without inferring field-level detail.

evidenceRequirements.name: Relm and HSB underwriting evidence
evidenceRequirements.requirement: The fetched Relm and HSB launch materials describe coverage but not minimum trace, audit, attestation, or human-oversight evidence.
evidenceRequirements.retain: Publicly undisclosed; recorded as not-publicly-disclosed rather than inferred.
